quick_facts.label: Conducting institution
quick_facts.value: BITSAT is conducted by Birla Institute of Technology and Science (BITS) Pilani for admission to its Integrated First Degree programmes.

quick_facts.label: Primary purpose
quick_facts.value: For the 2026–27 cycle, BITSAT was the computer-based entrance test for B.E., M.Sc. and B.Pharm. first-degree programmes at BITS Pilani’s Pilani, K K Birla Goa and Hyderabad campuses.

quick_facts.label: Academic eligibility baseline
quick_facts.value: For most B.E. and M.Sc. programmes, applicants need Class 12/10+2 with Physics, Chemistry and Mathematics, at least 75% aggregate in PCM and at least 60% in each of those subjects; B.Pharm., B.E. Environmental and Sustainability Engineering and M.Sc. Biological Sciences use PCB or PCM under the published rules.

quick_facts.label: Test structure (2026)
quick_facts.value: The published 2026 format was a three-hour computer-based multiple-choice test: Physics (30), Chemistry (30), English Proficiency (10), Logical Reasoning (20), and Mathematics or Biology (40), for 130 questions. Correct answers earned +3, incorrect answers −1, and unattempted questions earned 0.

quick_facts.label: Attempts (2026)
quick_facts.value: The 2026 cycle allowed up to two attempts, in Session 1 and Session 2; for a candidate who took both, BITS Pilani considered the higher score for admission.

quick_facts.label: Admission step beyond the test
quick_facts.value: Writing BITSAT alone does not complete the admission process: eligible candidates seeking a seat must also submit the separate admission/preference form with Class 12 marks, programme preferences and the required fee within the cycle’s published timetable.

sections.heading: What BITSAT is for
paragraphs: BITSAT is BITS Pilani’s entrance examination for its Integrated First Degree admissions. In the 2026–27 brochure, BITS Pilani states that admissions at its Pilani, K K Birla Goa and Hyderabad campuses are made through this computer-based online test, subject to the published eligibility and admission rules.

The programmes reachable through the BITSAT route include B.E. programmes, B.Pharm. and integrated M.Sc. programmes. Although this node uses “B.Tech” as a child label, BITS Pilani’s official programme nomenclature is generally “B.E.” rather than “B.Tech.”
bullets: Applicants should treat the BITS admissions website—not unofficial counselling or coaching sites—as the authoritative source for the active cycle.

The 2026 brochure also describes opportunities to apply to certain international 2+2 collaborative programmes using a BITSAT score; those pathways have separate programme-specific conditions and should not be assumed to be available unchanged in later cycles.

sections.heading: Who can take BITSAT
paragraphs: Eligibility is programme- and cycle-specific. Under the published BITSAT-2026 rules, candidates for most B.E. programmes and M.Sc. programmes needed a recognized 10+2 qualification with Physics, Chemistry, Mathematics, a language and one additional subject; they needed 75% aggregate in PCM and 60% in each PCM subject, counting theory and practical components.

For B.Pharm., B.E. Environmental and Sustainability Engineering, and M.Sc. Biological Sciences, the 2026 rules allowed Biology or Mathematics alongside Physics and Chemistry, with the same 75% aggregate and 60%-per-subject thresholds in the relevant three subjects. PCB entrants were required to have mathematics proficiency at Class 10 level.

For the 2026 examination, students appearing for Class 12 in 2026 or who passed it in 2025 were eligible, subject to the other rules. Students who passed Class 12 in 2024 or earlier, and students already enrolled in a BITS campus programme, were not eligible for BITSAT-2026. These year-of-passing rules should be checked afresh for a future cycle.
bullets: CAIE candidates must satisfy the required subjects at A Level; IB candidates must have them at Higher Level under the 2026 brochure.
Meeting the Class 12 marks threshold is required for admission even where a candidate has taken BITSAT; admission merit is based on the BITSAT score among eligible applicants.

sections.heading: Exam format and scoring
paragraphs: BITSAT-2026 was a three-hour, no-break computer-based test. It used four-option objective questions, with 130 regular questions across Physics, Chemistry, English Proficiency, Logical Reasoning, and Mathematics or Biology. Candidates could move among sections and revise answers during the test.

The 2026 marking scheme awarded three marks for each correct response, deducted one mark for each incorrect response, and gave no marks for an unattempted question. A candidate who answered all 130 regular questions without skipping any could choose to attempt 12 extra questions if time remained.

The test is delivered in English. BITS Pilani states that different candidates receive questions selected from a large question bank and that question sets are designed to be comparable in difficulty and content.
bullets: English Proficiency: 10 questions; Logical Reasoning: 20 questions.
Mathematics or Biology: 40 questions. Biology is available for the eligible B.Pharm., B.E. Environmental and Sustainability Engineering and M.Sc. Biological Sciences routes under the 2026 rules.
A calculator, mobile phone and other unauthorized electronic material are prohibited; the brochure states that a scratch pad is supplied at the test centre.

sections.heading: Application and admission workflow
paragraphs: The process has two distinct stages: register and apply to write BITSAT, then—if seeking admission—submit the separate BITS admission application with Class 12 marks and ordered programme preferences. The second step is essential; a BITSAT score by itself is not a completed seat application.

For the 2026 cycle, BITS Pilani offered Session 1 and Session 2, permitted an eligible candidate to take the test up to twice, and used the higher of the two scores for admission. Applicants selected test-city preferences, received a city allotment, booked a date and slot when available, and downloaded a hall ticket.

Dates, application charges, operating test cities, available programmes and preference-form deadlines are cycle-specific. The 2026 brochure labelled several schedule items tentative and directed candidates to the official admissions site for changes; future applicants should rely on the next cycle’s brochure and live notices rather than reuse 2026 dates or fees.
bullets: Register online through BITS Pilani’s official admissions process and complete the BITSAT application and payment.
Use the published edit window, if applicable, to correct permitted fields; the 2026 rules excluded some fields, including test-city preference, from later changes.
Book the test slot if the cycle requires booking, download the hall ticket, and appear at the allotted centre.
After the test, submit Class 12 marks, documents and programme/campus preferences through the First Degree admission form, then follow iteration, offer-acceptance and fee-payment notices.

sections.heading: Preparation approach grounded in the official syllabus
paragraphs: Start with the official BITSAT syllabus and build Class 11–12 command in the tested science and mathematics/biology subjects. BITS Pilani explicitly directs candidates to the detailed syllabus in its brochure and says that candidates may refer to NCERT textbooks for the content.

Preparation should also include English Proficiency and Logical Reasoning rather than treating BITSAT as only a PCM examination. The published syllabus includes grammar, vocabulary, reading comprehension and composition-related skills in English, plus verbal and non-verbal reasoning topics.

Because the 2026 paper required 130 questions in three hours and used negative marking, timed computer-based practice and disciplined question selection are practical priorities. Candidates should use full-length practice to learn pacing, review weaker syllabus areas after each attempt, and avoid blind guessing when the expected benefit is poor under the +3/−1 scheme.
bullets: Study from the official topic list first; use NCERT-aligned revision to cover the stated content base.
Practise with a keyboard and mouse before test day: BITS Pilani says basic computer familiarity is assumed.
Include mixed-section, three-hour mock sessions so that Physics, Chemistry, English, reasoning and Mathematics/Biology pacing is rehearsed together.
Review accuracy as well as attempt count, since wrong answers carry a one-mark penalty in the published 2026 scheme.

sections.heading: Programmes and what the score leads to
paragraphs: A qualifying BITSAT score is used in a merit-based admission process for BITS Pilani’s First Degree programmes at its Indian Pilani, Goa and Hyderabad campuses. The precise programme list varies by campus and cycle, but the 2026 brochure lists B.E. programmes, B.Pharm. at Pilani and Hyderabad, and integrated M.Sc. options including Biological Sciences, Chemistry, Economics, Mathematics, Physics, and Semiconductor and Nanoscience.

Students entering an integrated M.Sc. programme may apply for a dual degree in engineering after their first year; the 2026 brochure says this is competitive and based on performance at BITS. It is not an automatic engineering-seat guarantee. For M.Sc. Biological Sciences entrants with PCB input, the published options are restricted to B.E. Environmental and Sustainability Engineering and B.Pharm.

Published cut-offs are historical outcomes rather than guaranteed future targets. For context, BITS Pilani’s official page reports final programme- and campus-specific BITSAT cut-offs for the 2025–26 admission year on a 390-mark scale; selection thresholds can change with programme, campus, applicant performance and seat availability.
bullets: Main immediate academic outcomes: B.E. (the official BITS designation corresponding broadly to the engineering pathway), B.Pharm. and four-year integrated M.Sc. programmes.
Admission rankings are prepared from BITSAT scores for applicants who also meet the Class 12 eligibility requirements and submit the prescribed admission application.
Applicants should use official historical cut-offs only to understand past competition, not as a promise of an admission score for a later cycle.
